perturbation.original_text: 11. SURVIVAl Termination of the present agreement, at any time and for any reason whatsoever shall not extinguish its terms. The terms of this Non-Disclosure and Confidentiality Agreement will survive for 5 years, for the benefit of The Parties and their respective successors.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. SURVIVAl Termination of the present agreement, at any time and for any reason whatsoever shall not extinguish its terms. The terms of this Non-Disclosure and Confidentiality Agreement will survive for 6 months, for the benefit of The Parties and their respective successors.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the survival term to 6 months may be considered unreasonably short for protecting trade secrets, potentially violating Quebec's legal standards for trade secret protection, particularly if the trade secret has a longer lifespan.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Quebec Civil Code on Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1457 states the general duty not to cause damage to another, which can be interpreted to include the misappropriation of trade secrets. A 6-month survival period might be deemed insufficient to protect valuable trade secrets, making it easier for a party to cause damage by disclosing information after that period. The modified term could conflict with the implied obligation to act in good faith and protect confidential information for a reasonable period, potentially violating Quebec's legal standards for trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Code of Quebec - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Provincial civil code This article needs additional citations for verification . Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Civil Code of Quebec" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( March 2009 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) Civil Code of Quebec National Assembly of Quebec Citation CQLR c. CCQ-1991 Royal assent 8 December 1991 Commenced 1 January 1994 Legislative history Bill citation Bill 125 (34th Legislature, 1st session) Introduced by Gil Rémillard , Minister of Justice First reading 18 December 1990 Second reading 4 June 1991 Third reading 8 December 1991 Repeals Civil Code of Lower Canada Related legislation An Act respecting the implementation of the reform of the Civil Code (SQ 1992, c. 57) The Civil Code of Quebec ( CCQ ; French : Code civil du Québec , pronounced [kɔd sivil dy kebɛk] ) is the civil code in force in the Canadian province of Quebec , which came into effect on January 1, 1994. It replaced the Civil Code of Lower Canada ( French : Code civil du Bas-Canada ) enacted by the Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada in 1865, which had been in force since August 1, 1866. The Civil Code of Quebec governs a number of areas affecting relations between individuals under Quebec law. It deals with the main rules governing the law of persons, the family, succession, property and civil liability. It also contains rules of evidence in civil matters and Quebec private international law. Scope [ edit ] The Code's scope is summarized in its preliminary provision: The Civil Code of Québec , in harmony with the Charter of human rights and freedoms (chapter C-12) and the general principles of law, governs persons, relations between persons, and property. The Civil Code comprises a body of rules which, in all matters within the letter, spirit or object of its provisions, lays down the jus commune , expressly or by implication. In these matters, the Code is the foundation of all other laws, although other laws may complement the Code or make exceptions to it. The Civil Code is in essence a body of rules and regulations that, in all matters treated by or in the spirit or vein of its provisions, sets forth the jus commune , or the law that applies to all of Quebec, either in express or implied terms. For the matters handled by the Code, it acts as the foundation of all other adjacent laws, although other laws may supplement the Code or make exceptions to it. As the cornerstone of Quebec's legal system, the Civil Code is frequently amended in order to keep in step with the demands of modern society. Layout and significant changes [ edit ] The Civil Code of Quebec comprises over 3,000 articles and is structured into major divisions and subdivisions called books, titles, chapters and subsections. The Code is made up of ten books: Persons The Family Successions Property Obligations Prior Claims and Hypothecs Evidence Prescription Publication of Rights Private International Law Articles of the code are numbered in sequential order (1, 2, 3...) across all books, going up to Article 3168. Articles inserted in-between are numbered with a decimal (1, 1.1, 2, 3...). Articles with multiple paragraphs do not have the paragraphs individually numbered. The Code restatement of the civil law in Quebec as of the date of its adoption, including judicial interpretation of codal provisions, that included several significant changes from the former Code: broad privacy and personality rights protection, [ 1 ] the adoption of rules on patrimonies by appropriation [ 2 ] (analogous to, but conceptually different from, the law of trusts in common law jurisdictions), the introduction of a requirement for parties to conduct themselves in good faith [ 3 ] the ability to create unincorporated associations with the ability to sue and be sued [
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the survival term of the Non-Disclosure Agreement from 5 years to 6 months. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this timeframe is insufficient to protect trade secrets under Quebec law. The Law Explanation further elaborates that a shorter survival period might not adequately protect valuable trade secrets, thus facilitating damage through disclosure. Snippet 2 mentions the Civil Code of Quebec and its role in governing relations between individuals and property, implying obligations related to trade secrets and confidentiality. While neither snippet explicitly states a minimum survival period, the significant reduction coupled with the explanation that this reduction could violate Quebec trade secret protection laws creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. RETURN OF COMPANY INFORMATION At any time, upon one Party's request, the other Party shall promptly redeliver to the first Party the Company Information and all materials relating to or involving Company Information of the first Party (whether prepared by the party or otherwise), and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part regarding the Company Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. RETURN OF COMPANY INFORMATION At any time, upon one Party's request, the other Party shall redeliver to the first Party the Company Information and all materials relating to or involving Company Information of the first Party (whether prepared by the party or otherwise) within 90 days, and may retain copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part regarding the Company Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly' to 'within 90 days' and allowing retention of copies contradicts standard confidentiality obligations, potentially violating Quebec law regarding misuse of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Quebec Civil Code regarding abuse of rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 7 states that no right may be exercised with the intent of injuring another or in an excessive and unreasonable manner, contrary to the requirements of good faith. Allowing a party to retain copies of confidential information and delaying its return for 90 days could be seen as an abuse of rights if it causes damage or prejudice to the disclosing party, going against principles of good faith.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: hough other laws may complement the Code or make exceptions to it. The Civil Code is in essence a body of rules and regulations that, in all matters treated by or in the spirit or vein of its provisions, sets forth the jus commune , or the law that applies to all of Quebec, either in express or implied terms. For the matters handled by the Code, it acts as the foundation of all other adjacent laws, although other laws may supplement the Code or make exceptions to it. As the cornerstone of Quebec's legal system, the Civil Code is frequently amended in order to keep in step with the demands of modern society. Layout and significant changes [ edit ] The Civil Code of Quebec comprises over 3,000 articles and is structured into major divisions and subdivisions called books, titles, chapters and subsections. The Code is made up of ten books: Persons The Family Successions Property Obligations Prior Claims and Hypothecs Evidence Prescription Publication of Rights Private International Law Articles of the code are numbered in sequential order (1, 2, 3...) across all books, going up to Article 3168. Articles inserted in-between are numbered with a decimal (1, 1.1, 2, 3...). Articles with multiple paragraphs do not have the paragraphs individually numbered. The Code restatement of the civil law in Quebec as of the date of its adoption, including judicial interpretation of codal provisions, that included several significant changes from the former Code: broad privacy and personality rights protection, [ 1 ] the adoption of rules on patrimonies by appropriation [ 2 ] (analogous to, but conceptually different from, the law of trusts in common law jurisdictions), the introduction of a requirement for parties to conduct themselves in good faith [ 3 ] the ability to create unincorporated associations with the ability to sue and be sued [ 4 ] the ability to create hypothecs on movable property [ 5 ] the introduction of a central register for personal and movable real rights [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] the codification of the rules relating to private international law [ 9 ] History [ edit ] Adoption of the Civil Code of Lower Canada [ edit ] See also: Civil Code of Lower Canada The substantive law of the 1866 Civil Code of Lower Canada was derived primarily from the judicial interpretations of the law that had been in force to that date in Lower Canada. The work of the Commission on codification was also inspired by some of the modernizations found in the 1804 Napoleonic code . At the time of Canadian Confederation , the Civil Code of Lower Canada replaced most of the laws inherited from the Custom of Paris and incorporated some English law as it had been applied in Lower Canada such as the English law of trusts . The former Civil Code was also inspired by the Louisiana Civil Code , the Field Code movement in New York , and the Civil Code of the Canton of Vaud (1819). Revision process (1955–1991) [ edit ] In 1955, the Government of Quebec embarked on a reform of the Civil Code. [ 10 ] The Civil Code Revision Office was established, headed by Thibaudeau Rinfret , a former Chief Justice of Canada . [ 11 ] In 1960, the role of the Office was expanded to include the appointment of four codifiers to work on a definitive draft for the new Code. [ 12 ] In 1961, Rinfret stepped down from the Office, and was replaced by André Nadeau who served until his appointment to the Superior Court of Quebec in 1964. [ 11 ] Paul-André Crépeau was subsequently appointed to head the Office, where he served until 1977. [ 11 ] The reform process that led to the replacement of the Civil Code of Lower Canada by the Civil Code of Quebec was one of the largest legislative recodification undertakings in any civil law jurisdiction . The Office produced reports, held consultations, and presented a Draft Civil Code with commentaries to the Quebec National Assembly on 15 August 1977. perturbation.justification: The modification of the contract from requiring 'prompt' return of company information to allowing a 90-day delay and permitting the retention of copies directly undermines confidentiality obligations. The Law Explanation posits that retaining copies and delaying return could be seen as an abuse of rights if it causes damage, contradicting good faith principles. While the scraped snippets don't directly address confidentiality, the Law Explanation, combined with the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition, supports that the change introduces a contradiction by setting a timeframe that potentially violates confidentiality obligations under Quebec law.

perturbation.original_text: 8. LIMIT The Parties' obligations under this agreement shall not apply to any information required by law, regulation or court order to be disclosed to regulatory authorities and other governmental bodies, or which The Parties can document was available to the public or known to The Parties prior to the date first above written. These obligations shall cease as to any information which the disclosing Party can document thereafter became or becomes available to the public otherwise than as a result of a disclosure by the disclosing Party or was or is disclosed to the disclosing <PAGE> Party by a party unrelated to the disclosing Party that does not have a non-disclosure obligation with respect to it. If Company Information of the other Party is required to be disclosed by law, regulation or court order to regulatory authorities or other governmental bodies, The Party must promptly inform the other Party before disclosing such Company Information so that the other Party may have a reasonable opportunity to contest such requirement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. LIMIT The Parties' obligations under this agreement shall not apply to any information required by law, regulation or court order to be disclosed to regulatory authorities and other governmental bodies, or which The Parties can document was available to the public or known to The Parties prior to the date first above written. These obligations shall cease as to any information which the disclosing Party can document thereafter became or becomes available to the public otherwise than as a result of a disclosure by the disclosing Party or was or is disclosed to the disclosing <PAGE> Party by a party unrelated to the disclosing Party that does not have a non-disclosure obligation with respect to it. If Company Information of the other Party is required to be disclosed by law, regulation or court order to regulatory authorities or other governmental bodies, The Party has no obligation to inform the other Party before disclosing such Company Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to inform the other party before disclosing Company Information as required by law contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Quebec law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1375 of the Civil Code of Quebec states that every person is bound to exercise his rights in accordance with the requirements of good faith. Removing the obligation to inform the other party about legally required disclosures could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, especially if such disclosure could harm the other party's interests. It violates the implied duty of fairness and cooperation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: the Civil Code of Quebec was one of the largest legislative recodification undertakings in any civil law jurisdiction . The Office produced reports, held consultations, and presented a Draft Civil Code with commentaries to the Quebec National Assembly on 15 August 1977. [ 13 ] After further consultations during the 1980s, portions of the Book on the Law of the Family were adopted. [ 14 ] [ 15 ] The consultation process continued through to the early 1990s. Implementation (1991–1994) [ edit ] The bill to enact the new Code was introduced into the National Assembly of Quebec on 18 December 1990 by Gil Rémillard , who was then Quebec's Minister of Justice . It received royal assent on 8 December 1991. It did not come into force until 1 January 1994, as the necessary legislation to provide transitional rules determining what matters would be subject to the new Code was not passed until 1992. [ 16 ] Harmonization with federal law [ edit ] The Government of Canada has been undertaking a review of all federal laws that deal with private law to ensure that they take into consideration the terminology, concepts and institutions of Quebec civil law. [ 17 ] In that regard, the following Acts have been passed: Income Tax Amendments Act, 2000 [ 18 ] Federal Law—Civil Law Harmonization Act, No. 1 [ 19 ] Federal Law—Civil Law Harmonization Act, No. 2 [ 20 ] Federal Law—Civil Law Harmonization Act, No. 3 [ 21 ] As part of the first Harmonization Act, the Federal Law and Civil Law of the Province of Quebec Act was passed, which came into effect on 1 June 2001, [ 22 ] which: repealed the provisions of the CCLC relating to areas under federal jurisdiction (insofar as they had not been already displaced by other federal Acts) standardized the rules relating to marriage that are to apply in Quebec as though they formed part of the Civil Code It is estimated that, as of 2011, the federal harmonization project was 46% complete. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the obligation to inform the other party before disclosing Company Information as required by law, regulation, or court order. The Law Explanation states that Article 1375 of the Civil Code of Quebec requires every person to exercise rights in accordance with good faith. The explanation also highlights that removing the obligation to inform the other party about legally required disclosures could be interpreted as acting in bad faith. Scraped Law Snippet 2 contains the reference 'CCQ 1375' which points to the Civil Code of Quebec Article 1375, thus supporting the law explanation. Therefore, because the modified text removes a step which might be seen to be required by law, it creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13. APPLICABLE LAW AND VENUE This agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws applicable in the province of Quebec and the venue for any dispute arising from this agreement shall be the courts of competent jurisdiction in the district of Montreal, Quebec, Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. APPLICABLE LAW AND VENUE This agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws applicable in the province of Ontario and the venue for any dispute arising from this agreement shall be the courts of competent jurisdiction in the district of Montreal, Quebec, Canada.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying Ontario law while maintaining venue in Montreal (Quebec) creates a conflict of laws issue, potentially violating principles of procedural fairness and predictability in Quebec civil procedure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Quebec Code of Civil Procedure regarding jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_citation: Code of Civil Procedure, CQLR c C-25.01, art. 3135
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 3135 of the Civil Code discusses conflict of laws. The courts of Quebec have jurisdiction when the dispute has a sufficient connection with Quebec. Choosing Ontario law but Quebec venue undermines predictability and raises questions about which jurisdiction's procedural rules apply. This could lead to disputes about applicable laws and procedural fairness violating fundamental legal principles.

perturbation.justification: The altered contract specifies Ontario law while maintaining the venue in Montreal, Quebec. The Law Explanation indicates that this creates a conflict of laws issue under Article 3135 of the Civil Code, raising questions about procedural rules and potentially violating fundamental legal principles due to undermined predictability. Although no specific scraped law snippets are supportive of this, the Law Explanation provides the necessary justification.
